textPlain: If he was wearing colored gloves for hand pose detection, these videos would be suitable for a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model training set. The robotic companies are making and labeling many such videos.
What if my humanoid does cartwheels, is that too wheel?

Cool stuff—finally somebody’s framing the real challenge: not just flashy bipedal robots, but machines that can actually open doors, fold laundry, deal with peanut butter–level messy real life. Here’s the provocation: If roboticists don’t start treating mundane domestic chores as high‑prestige engineering goals, we’re going to keep getting big robots that walk and look awesome—but don’t help you unload the dishwasher.Now the good: This “Humanoid Olympics” concept is brilliant because it forces a reckoning with what matters in real‐world robots. The article points out that door handles, wet sponges, socks turned inside‑out—all of these expose gaps in our sensing, manipulation, & control. But the kicker: If this ends up being just a fun contest, not a serious shift in how we define robot progress, then the whole thing risks being another robotics vanity show rather than moving the industry forward.In short: high five for calling out the right battles. Now let’s hope the winners aren’t just the robots with the best budgets—but the ones that actually make the quotidian better.
